steps.title: Identify the horizontal asymptote from the graph
steps.content: The graph shows $y = f(x) + 2$. As $x \to -\infty$ (moving far left), the curve approaches a horizontal line at $y = 5$. This is the horizontal asymptote of the displayed function.

steps.title: Shift the asymptote to find the asymptote of $f(x)$
steps.content: Since the graph displays $y = f(x) + 2$, the asymptote of $f(x)$ itself is $5 - 2 = 3$. The vertical shift of $+2$ shifts the asymptote up by 2 units.

steps.title: Match the form $f(x) = -3^{x} + c$ to find $c$
steps.content: All answer choices have the form $f(x) = -3^{x} + c$. For this exponential form, as $x \to -\infty$, the term $-3^{x} \to 0$, so the horizontal asymptote is $y = c$. Since we need the asymptote at $y = 3$, we have $c = 3$, giving $f(x) = -3^{x} + 3$.

steps.title: Verify with a point on the graph
steps.content:
At $x = 1$, the graph shows $y \approx 2$. This means $f(1) + 2 = 2$, so $f(1) = 0$.

Testing choice B: $f(1) = -3^{1} + 3 = -3 + 3 = 0$ ✓

That matches choice B — B is correct.
